div.head: 174. Memorandum From the Under Secretary of State for Security Assistance, Science, and Technology (Bartholomew) to Secretary of State BakerSource: Department of State, Office of the Under Secretary for Arms Control, International Security Affairs, Lot 05D259, Records of James Timbie, US-Russia START I CFE December 1990. Secret.
div.p:
We met twice today for a total more than 6 hours,No memorandum of conversation was found. and will meet tomorrow at 10:00.No memorandum of conversation was found. Obukhov led their team; their military reinforcements get here tomorrow. The main points were: ○
PPCM. We pushed the position in your letterSee Document 166. and they pushed the position in Shev’s letter.See Document 168.
—
The most interesting development of the day was Obukhov’s offer: If we agree to the PPCM position in Shev’s letter, they would
agree not to inspect all heavy bombers, but only those from which long-range ALCMs have been tested. That would take us part way there on the B–2 (we want testing with long-range nuclear ALCMs to trigger inspections). —
On PPCM per se, neither side budged. I may try out the sweeteners tomorrow.
○
New heavy ICBM silos. They believe the Shev/Yazov letterSee Document 173. closes this issue on the basis discussed in New York. —
Obukhov made very clear that he had no flexibility to reopen an arrangement worked out by Ministers in New York. He said our persistence was causing a certain bitterness. —
He showed no interest in my proposal to put a provision into the treaty that the Joint Commission would address this issue in the event that a silo became inoperable through accident, or political changes forced relocation of silos.
○
Other issues. We also discussed two technical issues—the SS–N–18 and the throw weight package—without progress to report. Experts will push on these tomorrow.No memorandum of conversation was found.
As first day’s go, this was about average. Tomorrow I plan to get into ALCMs and bombers, data denial (if we have a position), and return to PPCM.
